instructionBlocks.text: Please check that this question paper contains 31 printed pages.
Please check that this question paper contains 39 questions.
Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.

15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.
This question paper contain 39 questions. All questions are compulsory.
The question paper is divided into three sections — A, B and C. Section A: Biology (30 marks), Section B: Chemistry (25 marks), Section C: Physics (25 marks).
The question paper has MCQs, VSAs, SAs, LAs and CBQs. Marks are given against each question.
There are case based questions (CBQs) with three sub-questions and are of 4 marks each.

Divide your answer sheet into three sections as per the question paper — Section A (Biology), Section B (Chemistry) and Section C (Physics). It is compulsory to answer each question in its respective section.
There is no overall choice in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in few questions. Only one of the choices in such questions must be attempted.

sections.title: Section A
sections.subtitle: Biology
sections.instructions: Answer the Biology questions in this section. Instructions are given with each question, wherever necessary.

sections.title: Section B
sections.subtitle: Chemistry
sections.instructions: Answer the Chemistry questions in this section. Marks are given against each question.

sections.title: Section C
sections.subtitle: Physics
sections.instructions: Answer the Physics questions in this section. Internal choice, if any, must be attempted only once.

questions.rawText: If the stomatal pore is open, it indicates which situation from the following ?

content.text: The guard cells have shrunk.
The water comes out of the guard cells.
Large amount of water is lost through stomata.
The guard cells have swelled due to inflow of water.

(i) 2KClO3 ---> 2KCl + 3O2
(ii) MgO + H2O ---> Mg(OH)2
(iii) 2H2O ---> 2H2 + O2
(iv) Zn + CuSO4 ---> ZnSO4 + Cu

content.text: (i) and (iii)
(iii) and (iv)
(i) and (iv)
(ii) and (iii)

Sexual reproduction by division of cells into many cells

(i) Oxidation of carbon dioxide to carbohydrates
(ii) Conversion of light energy into chemical energy
(iii) Absorption of light energy by chlorophyll
(iv) Conversion of hydrogen and oxygen into water

content.text: (i) and (ii)
(ii) and (iii)
(iii) and (iv)
(i) and (iv)

questions.rawText: Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow.

passage.text: A middle-aged person is facing some cognitive changes in himself. His early symptoms included mild forgetfulness. For example, forgetting where he left his mobile or failing to find words to complete his sentences. He was facing problems in driving a car, climbing up and down the stairs, inserting a plug into an outlet. He slowly developed a sad mood and anxiety.

(iii) Mention the main physical feature of the male germ cell that helps it move towards the female germ cell.

(ii) Name the surgical methods to prevent pregnancy.
(iii) Give one example each of sexually transmitted diseases caused by bacteria and virus.

questions.rawText: A student notices that her silver jewellery turned dull and had a grey-black coating over it after wearing for a few months. What results in the change in colour of the silver metal ?

The jewellery comes in contact with air, moisture and acids and corrodes.
Dust particles over the jewellery change its colour.
Its colour changes due to rusting.

(ii) Aluminium oxide is considered as an amphoteric oxide.

questions.rawText: Electricity passed through aqueous solution of salt.

passage.text: In the given diagram, when electricity is passed through an aqueous solution of a common salt, a substance ‘Z’ is produced, along with the evolution of gases ‘X’ and ‘Y’. When a burning matchstick is brought near the gas ‘Y’, it burns with a pop sound, whereas ‘X’ is used for disinfecting drinking water. When ‘X’ is passed through slaked lime, an insoluble substance ‘A’ is produced.

content.text: Write the names of the gases ‘X’ and ‘Y’.
Write the balanced chemical equation for the formation of substance ‘Z’.
What happens :
(I) If a drop of red litmus solution is added to the aqueous solution of substance ‘Z’ ?
(II) If CO2 gas is passed through ammoniacal solution of aqueous NaCl ?
Identify the substance ‘A’ and write the balanced chemical equation of the reaction which takes place in its preparation.

questions.rawText: Chemical equations for carbon compounds.

content.text: A carbon compound ‘P’ of molecular formula C2H6O is found to be neutral when tested with red and blue litmus. A gas that burns with a pop sound is produced when sodium metal reacts with compound ‘P’.
(i) Identify the compound ‘P’.
(ii) The carbon compound ‘P’ is heated with conc. H2SO4 at 443 K to produce compound ‘Q’.
(I) Write the chemical equation for the reaction. (II) What is the role of conc. H2SO4 in this reaction ?
(iii) What is likely to be observed on heating compound ‘P’ with ethanoic acid with an acid as a catalyst ? Write the chemical equation for the reaction.
(i) Write the structural formula of benzene.
(ii) State the reason why carbon can neither form C4+ cation nor C4– anion.
(iii) What type of bond is formed between carbon and hydrogen ?
(iv) How is ethanol converted to ethanoic acid ?
CH3COOC2H5 + NaOH -> CH3COONa + C2H5OH

questions.rawText: Read the passage on endocrine glands and answer the questions that follow.

passage.text: The endocrine system consists of glands that secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream. These hormones regulate metabolism, growth, and development. For instance, thyroid gland secretes thyroxine which regulates carbohydrate, protein, and fat metabolism. Pancreas secretes insulin which regulates blood sugar levels. Adrenal glands secrete adrenaline to prepare the body for emergency situations.

content.text: Name the hormone secreted by the thyroid gland and state its primary function.
Which hormone is responsible for regulating blood sugar levels in the human body ?
Explain the physiological responses in the human body when adrenaline is secreted into the blood.
What is a feedback mechanism ? Explain it with reference to the regulation of insulin secretion.

questions.rawText:
Answer either (a) or (b) from How do Organisms Reproduce ?
(a) Describe female reproductive system.
(b) Explain various contraceptive methods.

content.text: (i) Name the parts of the female reproductive system where egg is produced, and where fertilization takes place.
(ii) Describe the changes that occur in the uterus of human female if the egg is not fertilized.
(iii) What is placenta ? Explain its function.
(i) Classify various contraceptive methods used to avoid pregnancy under three main categories, giving one example of each.
(ii) Mention the health advantages of using barrier methods over surgical methods.

stem.text: Copper vessels get a green coating over them when exposed to moist air for a long time. This green coating is due to the formation of :

questions.rawText:
Assertion (A) : Carbon forms covalent bonds with hydrogen, chlorine, oxygen, and other elements.
Reason (R) : Carbon has four valence electrons and shares them to achieve a stable octet configuration.

assertion.text: Carbon forms covalent bonds with hydrogen, chlorine, oxygen, and other elements.

reason.text: Carbon has four valence electrons and shares them to achieve a stable octet configuration.

content.text: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.

questions.rawText: Explain displacement reactions in copper sulphate solution.

passage.text: A student added zinc granules to blue copper sulphate solution in a test tube. After some time, she noticed that the blue color of the solution faded and a reddish-brown deposit was formed at the bottom.

content.text: Explain the cause of the decolorization of the solution.
Write a balanced chemical equation for the displacement reaction involved.

questions.rawText:
Answer either (a) or (b) about elements and oxides :
(a) Define ionic bond and write the electron-dot structure of Sodium chloride.
(b) Give reasons why ionic compounds have high melting points, and why they conduct electricity in molten state but not in solid state.

content.text: Explain the formation of Sodium Chloride (NaCl) from sodium and chlorine atoms using electron dot representation.
Give reasons :
(i) Ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points.
(ii) Ionic compounds conduct electricity in molten or aqueous state, but not in solid state.

questions.rawText: When magnesium ribbon is burnt in air, a dazzling white flame is produced along with a white powder. Name the powder and write the chemical equation for the reaction.

stem.text: When magnesium ribbon is burnt in air, a dazzling white flame is produced along with a white powder. Name the powder and identify the type of chemical reaction.
Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction. Is magnesium oxidized or reduced in this reaction ?

questions.rawText: Read the passage on Plaster of Paris and answer the questions that follow.

passage.text: Plaster of Paris (POP) is a white powder chemically known as calcium sulphate hemihydrate. It is prepared by heating gypsum (calcium sulphate dihydrate) at 373 K in a kiln. POP is used by doctors to support fractured bones in the correct position. On mixing with water, it changes to gypsum once again, forming a hard solid mass.

content.text: Write the chemical formula of Plaster of Paris.
Why should Plaster of Paris be stored in moisture-proof containers ?
Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction of Plaster of Paris with water to form gypsum.
Name the raw material used for the preparation of POP and write the equation for its preparation when heated at 373 K.

questions.rawText:
Answer either (a) or (b) from Carbon and its Compounds :
(a) Explain esterification and saponification.
(b) Describe homologous series and clean action of soaps.

content.text: (i) What are esters ? How are they prepared ? Write the chemical equation for esterification of ethanoic acid and ethanol.
(ii) Write the chemical equation for saponification of ethyl ethanoate with sodium hydroxide.
(i) What is homologous series ? State any two characteristics of homologous series.
(ii) Explain the cleansing action of soaps with the help of micelle formation.

questions.rawText: Read the passage on household electrical appliances and answer the questions.

passage.text: An electric oven rated 2 kW, 220 V is operated in a domestic electric circuit that has a current rating of 5 A. The total power consumed by domestic appliances must not exceed the line limits, otherwise overloading occurs, causing the fuse to melt.

content.text: What is overloading in a domestic electric circuit ?
Write the formula relating electric power (P), potential difference (V) and current (I).
Calculate the current drawn by the electric oven. What will happen if it is connected to a 5 A line ? Explain.
Why should a fuse of appropriate rating be connected in series with the live wire of a domestic circuit ?

metadata.subject: Science

questions.rawText:
Answer either (a) or (b) from Magnetic Effects of Electric Current :
(a) Explain solenoid magnetic field.
(b) Describe electromagnetic induction.

content.text: (i) What is a solenoid ? Draw the pattern of magnetic field lines of a current-carrying solenoid.
(ii) State two ways by which the strength of magnetic field inside a solenoid can be increased.
(iii) What is an electromagnet ? How does it differ from a permanent magnet ?
(i) State Fleming's Right-Hand Rule for finding the direction of induced current.
(ii) Explain the phenomenon of electromagnetic induction with the help of a labeled experimental diagram.
(iii) Name any two devices that work on the principle of electromagnetic induction.
